meta.title: W164 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes M-Class W164 engine — OM642, M272, M113, M273, M156, OM629. Real UK costs, honest reliability data. Compare prices from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes M-Class W164 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W164 engine typically costs £3,000–£14,000+ supply only, depending on the engine — the volume OM642 diesel sits at £3,000–£5,500, while the M156 AMG unit ranges from £8,000–£14,000+. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W164 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642.940 V6 diesel (ML 320 CDI) and the M113.964 V8 petrol (ML 500, pre-facelift) are the most dependable. The OM642 is a robust, proven V6 diesel that regularly exceeds 200,000 miles with proper maintenance — though oil cooler seal leaks are a known issue. The M113 is one of Mercedes' most bulletproof V8s with no balance-shaft issues.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes M-Class W164?

acceptedAnswer.text: M272/M273 balance shaft failure on pre-2008 V6/V8 petrol engines, OM642 oil cooler seal leaks (V6 diesel), OM642 timing chain wear on higher-mileage examples, AIRMATIC air suspension failure, and — unrelated to the engine — issues with the SBC (Sensotronic Brake Control) system on early cars.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes M-Class W164 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the engine and the year. The OM642 V6 diesel and M113 V8 are genuinely robust and well-documented. The M272 V6 and M273 V8 have balance shaft issues on pre-2008 engines — if you're buying one, check the engine serial number against Mercedes' affected range. The ML 500 with M113 V8 and diesel models were NOT affected by balance shaft issues.

mainEntity.name: Which W164 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but pre-2008 M272 V6 (ML 350) and M273 V8 (ML 500) engines need careful checking for balance shaft history. If you're buying one, check the engine serial number — if it starts with '272' or '273' and is pre-2008, it may be affected. The ML 500 with M113 V8 and diesel models were NOT affected. The OM629 V8 diesel is also a specialist engine with higher running costs.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W164 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Balance shaft rattle or check engine light with timing codes (M272/M273), oil leaks around the engine valley or oil in the wiring harness (OM642), rough idle or diesel smell (OM642 injector issues), or suspension warning lights (AIRMATIC failure) are key warnings. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes M-Class W164 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £8,000+ W164 (especially a V8 or AMG), a £3,000–£5,500 recon is 38–69% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £4,000 example with air suspension issues, a £3,000+ recon may exceed the car's worth — walk away. Our 70% Mercedes threshold is the line. For the M156 AMG, the maths is different — if you can afford a £15,000+ ML 63, you can afford the maintenance.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes M-Class W164 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642 V6 diesel and M113 V8 can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing. The M272/M273 are typically good for 150,000+ miles if the balance shaft has been addressed. The M156 AMG is typically good for 80,000–120,000 miles if maintained to AMG standards. The OM629 V8 diesel is rarer but can exceed 150,000 miles with specialist maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W164 and X164?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both are built on the same platform. The W164 is the 5-door M-Class SUV (2005–2011, this page) with five seats, while the X164 is the GL-Class (2006–2012) with a longer body and third-row seating for seven passengers. They share the same engines and many components — but the GL-Class is larger and heavier. Costs vary slightly due to the additional weight and larger body — see the X164 generation page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes M-Class W164 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the block. Tell us your variant (e.g., ML 320 CDI, ML 350, ML 500) and registration year, and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes M-Class W164 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W164 SUV — from the OM642 V6 diesel to the M272 and M273 V6/V8 petrols, and the fire-breathing M156 V8 in the ML 63 AMG. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the balance-shaft issues that still haunt early V6-powered cars. Backed by real UK W164 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes M-Class W164 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W164 SUV specifically — from the entry-level V6 diesel to the naturally aspirated V8 in the ML 63 AMG.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM642 V6 diesel: 85% confidence — widely regarded as a robust engine, though oil cooler seal leaks and timing chain issues affect some examples. M113 V8: 85% confidence — one of Mercedes' most durable V8s. M272 V6: 75% confidence — early units suffer from balance shaft failure. M273 V8: 75% confidence — related balance shaft/idler sprocket issues. M156: 75% confidence — high-performance AMG V8 with higher running costs but good reliability for a 510 hp engine. OM629 V8 diesel: 70% confidence — rare and specialist, with higher running costs.

overview.intro: The W164 is the second-generation M-Class, launched in January 2005 at the North American International Auto Show in Detroit. Built at the US plant in Tuscaloosa, Alabama, it replaced the W163 and introduced a unibody construction instead of the ladder-type frame used previously. The engine range spans the OM642 V6 diesel in two output levels, the M272 V6 petrol, the M113 and M273 V8 petrols, the M156 V8 in the ML 63 AMG, and the OM629 V8 diesel. A 2008 facelift brought revised styling, interior updates, and mechanical improvements.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W164 (unibody, shared with GL-Class X164) • Predecessor: W163 • Successor: W166 • Sibling platform: GL-Class (X164).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W164 M-Class generated real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM642 diesel and M272 petrol as these cars enter enthusiast-owner territory.

items.quote: The 3.0-litre V6 diesel OM642 delivers 224 hp with 510 Nm of torque, combining strong performance with reasonable economy. It's a robust, proven engine that regularly exceeds 200,000 miles with proper maintenance — though oil cooler seal leaks are a known issue. The ML 350 BlueTEC (OM642.820) from 2009 onwards is equally refined.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the best combination of performance, economy and reliability in the W164 range.

items.quote: The 5.0-litre M113 V8 is one of the most bulletproof petrol engines Mercedes ever built — 306 hp of smooth, naturally aspirated power, no balance-shaft issues, no timing-chain worries. It's the W164's sleeper pick — a V8 that sounds wonderful and keeps going.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 refinement without the M273's balance-shaft risk.

items.quote: The M272 V6 is a great engine — but early units (2005–2008) suffer from a well-documented balance shaft gear failure. The teeth wear away, causing the timing to jump and potentially destroying the engine. If your engine serial number starts with '272', check whether it's in the affected range. The ML 500 with M113 V8 and diesel models were NOT affected.
items.whoItsFor: buyers willing to check engine numbers and budget for balance shaft replacement, or those buying post-2008 examples.

items.quote: The 6.2-litre M156 V8 is a masterpiece — 510 hp of naturally aspirated AMG thunder, at the time the world's most powerful naturally aspirated 8-cylinder standard-production engine. But when it fails, it fails expensively. Camshaft adjusters, lifters, and the sheer cost of AMG-specific parts mean replacement bills frequently hit five figures.
items.whoItsFor: collectors and enthusiasts with deep pockets.

items.quote: The ML 280 CDI offers 190 hp with the same robust OM642 V6 diesel as the more powerful ML 320 CDI. It's cheaper to buy, cheaper to insure, and cheaper to replace than the petrol alternatives — the sweet spot in the W164 diesel range for most UK buyers.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want diesel economy and reliability without the V8's fuel consumption.

items.quote: The 4.0-litre V8 diesel is rare in the W164 — 260 hp and an effortless 560 Nm of torque. It's the ultimate W164 for long-distance towing, offering V8 refinement with diesel economy. But it's a specialist engine — parts are harder to find and labour is more intensive than the V6 diesel.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who need serious towing capability and are prepared for specialist maintenance.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Oil cooler seal leaks, timing chain
Oil cooler seal leaks, timing chain

rows.ownershipVerdict: Highest Risk — great engine, but check balance shaft.
Best Petrol — bulletproof V8, no major issues.
Facelift V8 — powerful, but check engine number.
Most Expensive — AMG masterpiece, AMG bills.
V8 Diesel — rare, powerful, specialist territory.

economicsRule.title: The W164 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,000–£5,500 OM642 recon against a £5,000 W164 sits at 60–110% of the car's value. On a sub-£4,500 W164, that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £8,000+ example (particularly a V8 or AMG), the same recon drops to a routine 38–69% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first. For the M156 AMG, the maths is different — if you can afford a £15,000+ ML 63, you can afford the maintenance.

problems.title: M272/M273 Balance Shaft Failure
problems.rootCause: The M272 V6 and M273 V8 engines have a well-documented balance shaft gear failure. The teeth wear away, causing the timing to jump and potentially destroying the engine. The V8 (M273) has a related idler sprocket issue even though it has no balance shaft. This only affects engines with serial numbers starting with "272" or "273". The ML 500 with M113 V8 and diesel models were NOT affected.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a pre-2008 W164 with an M272 or M273 engine, check the engine serial number against Mercedes' affected range. If it's affected, budget for balance shaft replacement or walk away. A £1,200–£2,200 preventative repair is far cheaper than the £3,500–£6,500 replacement that follows a complete failure.

cta.label: Compare M272/M273 engine prices for your W164 ML 350/500 →

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leaks
problems.affectedModels: ML 280 CDI, ML 320 CDI, ML 350 BlueTEC (OM642 V6 diesel).
problems.rootCause: The OM642's oil cooler seals degrade over time, allowing oil to leak into the valley of the V6 engine. This is a labour-intensive repair as the intake manifold must be removed to access the seals.
problems.recommendation: Check for oil leaks around the engine valley on any OM642-powered W164 — a £700–£1,200 repair is far cheaper than the £3,000–£5,500 replacement that follows oil starvation. Also check for oil in the wiring harness — this is another known OM642 issue.

cta.label: Compare OM642 engine prices for your W164 ML 280/320 CDI →

problems.title: W164 Airmatic Suspension Failure
problems.affectedModels: W164 models with AIRMATIC air suspension (optional on most models).
problems.rootCause: The W164's AIRMATIC air suspension system — the rubber air springs perish over time and crack, allowing air to leak. The compressor, ride height sensors, and valve block can also fail.
problems.recommendation: Have the air suspension inspected on any W164 — a £200–£500 inspection is far cheaper than the £1,500–£3,000+ replacement that follows a complete system failure. Check for the car sitting low overnight or unevenly, as these are early warning signs.

cta.label: Compare W164 air suspension repair quotes →

problems.title: OM642 Timing Chain Wear
problems.affectedModels: ML 280 CDI, ML 320 CDI, ML 350 BlueTEC (OM642 V6 diesel).
problems.rootCause: Some OM642 engines can suffer from timing chain wear, particularly if oil changes have been neglected or the wrong oil has been used. The chain can stretch, causing poor running and eventually catastrophic failure.
problems.recommendation: On any OM642-powered W164 over 100,000 miles, have the timing chain inspected at every service — a £800–£1,500 repair is far cheaper than the £3,000–£5,500 replacement that follows a skipped chain. Use only Mercedes-spec oil to minimise wear.

cta.label: Compare OM642 timing chain repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: W164 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — W164 SUV shares its platform with the X164 GL-Class, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required. M156 AMG costs reflect AMG-specific parts and specialist labour requirements.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W164 SUV only. X164 GL-Class shares some of these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page, as it features a longer body with third-row seating.
